titleStmt.title: Enhancement of microwave squeezing via parametric down-conversion in a superconducting quantum circuit

funder.orgName: Zhejiang Province Program for Science and Technology
National Natural Science Foundation of China
National Key Research and Development Program of China

affiliation.orgName: Zhejiang Province Key Laboratory of Quantum Technology and Device
State Key Laboratory of Modern Optical Instrumentation
Zhejiang Province Key Laboratory of Quantum Technology and Device
State Key Laboratory of Modern Optical Instrumentation

analytic.title: Enhancement of microwave squeezing via parametric down-conversion in a superconducting quantum circuit

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We propose an experimentally accessible superconducting quantum circuit, consisting of two coplanar waveguide resonators (CWRs), to enhance the microwave squeezing via parametric down-conversion (PDC). In our scheme, the two CWRs are nonlinearly coupled through a superconducting quantum interference device embedded in one of the CWRs. This is equivalent to replacing the transmission line in a flux-driven Josephson parametric amplifier (JPA) by a CWR, which makes it possible to drive the JPA by a quantized microwave field. Owing to this design, the PDC coefficient can be considerably increased to be about tens of megahertz, satisfying the strong-coupling condition. Using the Heisenberg-Langevin approach, we numerically show the enhancement of the microwave squeezing in our scheme. In contrast to the JPA, our proposed system becomes stable around the critical point and can generate stronger transient squeezing. In addition, the strong-coupling PDC can be used to engineer the photon blockade.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Squeezing, i.e., the reduction of quantum fluctuations in one quadrature component at the expense of increasing fluctuations in the other canonically conjugate variable, is one of the extraordinary effects in quantum optics [1][2][3]. Owing to the promising applications in, e.g., precision measurement [4][5][6], quantum key distribution [7,8], engineering matter interactions [9][10][11] and improving the efficiency of heat engines [12,13], the generation of squeezed light has attracted much attention. Up to now, numerous schemes for squeezing have been proposed, typically using the optomechanical interaction [14][15][16], the four-wave mixing [2], and the degenerate parametric down-conversion (PDC) [3]. Here, the degenerate PDC involves a three-wave mixing process of generating a lower-energy photon pair with the same frequency by splitting a higher-energy photon via a nonlinear medium [17].

On the other hand, fast developments of the superconducting quantum circuits (SQCs) have greatly facilitated the progress in quantum information processing (see. e.g., [18][19][20][21]). Since the SQCs based on Josephson junctions can be designed and fabricated to tailor their characteristics for various purposes, they have also been used as the platforms for exploring quantum-optics phenomena in the microwave domain [19]. For instance, electromagnetically induced transparency and Autler-Townes splitting [22][23][24][25], sideband transitions [26,27],quantum entanglement [28][29][30][31][32][33], Bloch-Siegert shifts [34,35] and superradiant quantum phase transitions [36][37][38][39] were demonstrated using the SQCs. Also, the generation of squeezed microwaves via, e.g., degenerate fourwave mixing and degenerate PDC was proposed theoretically and later realized experimentally [40][41][42][43][44][45]. Most importantly, the advantage of easy tunability in SQCs makes it promising to demonstrate and manipulate more quantum-optics phenomena in the microwave domain.

In this paper, we propose an experimentally feasible scheme to obtain squeezed microwaves via quantized-fielddriven PDC in a SQC. The considered system is composed of two superconducting coplanar waveguide resonators (CWRs), denoted as CWR-A and CWR-B, which are inductively coupled to each other by a superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) embedded in CWR-A. The magnetic flux threading the SQUID loop contains the externally applied magnetic flux and the quantized magnetic flux generated by the current in CWR-B. This is equivalent to replacing the transmission line in a flux-driven Josephson parametric amplifier (JPA) with a CWR (i.e., the CWR-B), which makes it possible to drive the JPA by a quantized microwave field. A similar method was also used in the optical parametric amplifier to enhance the squeezing by embedding the nonlinear crystal in an optical cavity [46], where the nonlinear crystal and the optical cavity play similar roles as the nonlinear CWR-A (i.e., the JPA) and the CWR-B in our proposed system, respectively. The generated quantized magnetic flux modulates the phase across the SQUID and gives rise to the mutual coupling between the two resonators. The SQUID usually works in the phase regime where the phase degree of freedom dominates. When the external magnetic field is much stronger than the generated quantized magnetic field, the coupled SQC system can be described by a PDC Hamiltonian, where the classical drive field in the JPA is replaced by a quantized drive field. With this quantized-field-driven PDC Hamiltonian, we study the microwave squeezing in the proposed system. In fact, due to the nonlinearity of the Josephson junction, a small SQUID is a highly nonlinear system. Therefore, we can use it to explore the high-order nonlinear effect of the SQUID on the microwave squeezing in our SQC system.

Around a critical drive strength, our proposed system is stable and can produce optimum steady-state squeezing. This is different from a flux-driven JPA [43], which can have the same degree of steady-state squeezing, but is unstable around the critical point, yielding the optimum steady-state squeezing experimentally inaccessible. Moreover, for the JPA, its transient squeezing cannot exceed the steady-state squeezing, while our scheme does not have this limitation and stronger transient squeezing can be achieved (cf. Sec. III). Also, compared with the microwave squeezing in Refs. [44,45], our proposal has distinct advantages. First, the design we present here is nondispersive and directly interacted. This is in contrast to other proposals based on dispersive and indirect couplings via couplers [44,45]. While in those works the nonlinear coupling strength (which is only tens of kilohertz) is much limited by the dispersive condition, our setup can increase the nonlinear coupling strength by three orders of magnitude. This promises an appreciable enhancement of the microwave squeezing in the proposed circuit. Second, our setup with the SQUID can be easily fabricated as well, since the needed technology has been maturely utilized for other experiments [47]. In addition, our scheme may have other potential applications in quantum technologies. For example, the nonlinear coupling strength in our scheme is larger than the decay rates of CWRs (usually smaller than one megahertz [48]), so a strong coupling can be realized between the two CWRs. Contrary to the weakcoupling regime, the photon blockade becomes accessible in this strong-coupling regime [49].
div.formula: ( ) J e E D E F D E F (c) C in C out C J C 0 C 0 L 0 L 0 -l a x l a (a) CWR-A CWR-B (b)

div.head: II. PROPOSED CIRCUIT
div.p: As illustrated in Fig. 1(a), the proposed SQC system is composed of two CWRs (i.e., CWR-A and CWR-B), where both resonators are inductively coupled to each other via a SQUID embedded in CWR-A. Below we first derive CWR-A's Hamiltonian and then give the Hamiltonian of the proposed SQC system.

div.head: A. Nonlinear resonator
div.p: Due to the SQUID embedded, CWR-A is a nonlinear resonator, which can act as a JPA in the regime with appropriate parameters [43]. We assume CWR-A to be of a length 2l a and decompose it into lumped-circuit elements, as shown in Fig. 1(c). For simplicity and without loss of generality, we also assume that the two resonators have the same capacitance C 0 per unit length and the same inductance L 0 per unit length. When the SQUID is excluded, the Lagrangian of the bare resonator for CWR-A can be written as
div.formula: L a = l a -l a C 0 2 Φ2 (x, t) - [∂ x Φ(x, t)] 2 2L 0 dx,(1)
div.p: where Φ(x, t) = t -∞ V(x, t )dt is the magnetic flux and V(x, t ) is the voltage. For a symmetric SQUID with C J1 = C J2 = C J /2 and E J1 = E J2 = E J /2, the effective Josephson energy is E J (Φ e ) = E J cos(πΦ e /Φ 0 ), where Φ 0 = h/(2e) is the flux quantum. The total magnetic flux Φ e threading the SQUID loop is composed of the externally applied magnetic flux Φ e and the magnetic flux Φ b generated by the current in CWR-B, i.e., Φ e = Φ e + Φ b , where the small magnetic flux induced by the self inductance of the SQUID loop is neglected. Under the condition Φ e Φ b , the change of the effective Josephson energy E J (Φ e ) due to the magnetic flux Φ b can be linearly approximated as
div.formula: E J (Φ e ) ≈ E J (Φ e )[1 - tan(πΦ e /Φ 0 )(πΦ b /Φ 0 )].
div.p: This approximation is reasonable because 0.25 ≤ Φ e /Φ 0 ≤ 0.45 and Φ b /Φ 0 ∼ 1 × 10 -3 in our paper.

The Lagrangian of the SQUID takes the standard form L S = C J δ2 /2-E J (Φ e ) 1-cos(2πδ/Φ 0 ) , where δ = Φ(x + J , t)-Φ(x - J , t) is the phase drop of the SQUID located at x = x J [see Fig. 1(c)]. Another important parameter of the SQUID is the effective charge energy E C = (2e) 2 /(2C J ). When the SQUID is in the phase regime, i.e., E J (Φ e ) E C , we expand the cosine potential of the SQUID up to the fourth order of 2πδ/Φ 0 . The Lagrangian L S can be approximately written as L S = L (2) S + L (3) S + L (4) S , where
div.formula: L (2) S = C J 2 δ2 - δ 2 2L J , L (3) S = δ 2 2L J π Φ b Φ 0 tan πΦ e Φ 0 , L (4) S = δ 4 24L J 2π Φ 0 2 ,(2)
div.p: with the Josephson inductance L J defined by 1/L J = (2π/Φ 0 ) 2 E J (Φ e ). The quality factor of the resonator for CWR-A is assumed to be sufficiently high, so we can consider the limiting case C in,out → 0, which corresponds to the openended boundary condition [50] ∂
div.formula: x Φ(x, t)| x=-l a = ∂ x Φ(x, t)| x=l a = 0,(3)
div.p: where C in and C out are the capacitances of the input and output capacitors at the two ends of CWR-A. Moreover, according to Kirchhoff's current law, around x = x J , we have 1
div.formula: L 0 ∂ x Φ(x, t)| x=x - J = 1 L 0 ∂ x Φ(x, t)| x=x + J = C J δ + δ L J ,(4)
div.p: due to the presence of the SQUID in the resonator [51].

First, we study the normal modes of CWR-A and quantize the Hamiltonian related to the linear Lagrangian L a = L a + L (2) S , where higher-order nonlinear terms in the Lagrangian L S are ignored. As shown in Appendix A, by using the method of separating variables under the conditions in Eqs. ( 3) and (4), the linear Lagrangian L a of CWR-A can be written as
div.formula: L a = m [C Σ φ2 m /2-φ 2 m /2L m ].
div.p: Here C Σ ≡ 2C 0 l a +C J is the total capacitance and L m is the effective inductance related to the mth mode of CWR-A. Both the flux amplitude φ m and the spatial mode function µ m (x) of the mth mode satisfy Φ(x, t) = m φ m (t)µ m (x). With a Legendre transformation, the Hamiltonian H a of CWR-A is obtained as
div.formula: H a = m p 2 m 2C Σ + φ 2 m 2L m ,(5)
div.p: where p m = C Σ φm is the canonical momentum of the mth mode, conjugate to the canonical coordinate φ m . We treat the canonical variables φ m and p m as quantum operators satisfying the commutation relation [φ n , p m ] = i δ nm and can then write them as
div.formula: φ m = 2C Σ ω m (a † m + a m ), p m = i C Σ ω m 2 (a † m -a m ),(6)
div.p: where a † m and a m are the creation and annihilation operators of the mth resonator mode and ω m = 1/ √ C Σ L m is the resonant frequency of this mode. If we only focus on the fundamental mode of the resonator (i.e., m = 1), the Hamiltonian (5) when setting = 1 is reduced to H a = ω a a † a, with a ≡ a 1 and ω a ≡ ω 1 . The resonant frequency ω a satisfies ω a = k 1 υ, where k 1 is the wave vector of the fundamental mode and υ = 1/ √ C 0 L 0 is the group velocity.

div.head: B. Hamiltonian of the proposed system
div.p: We consider CWR-B to be a λ/2-type resonator and focus on its fundamental mode as well, i.e., the λ/2 mode. The Hamiltonian of this mode can be written as
div.formula: H b = ω b b † b, with ω b = πυ/(2l b
div.p: ) and b † (b) being the resonant frequency and the creation (annihilation) operator of the mode, respectively. Here, 2l b is the length of the resonator. The quantized current in the waveguide of CWR-B reaches its maximum [52]. Correspondingly, the magnetic field generated by this current reads B = µ 0 I/(2πr), where µ 0 is the vacuum permeability and r is the radial distance away from the waveguide of CWR-B. The SQUID loop can be designed as a rectangular loop with length s and width b 1 , and it has a distance b 2 away from the waveguide of the resonator CWR-B [see Fig. 1(b)]. Therefore, in the SQUID loop, the part of the quantized magnetic flux generated by the current in CWR-B can be written as which drives the JPA (i.e., the CWR-A) to produce microwave squeezing (cf. Sec. III), where
div.formula: I = √ ω b /(2L 0 l b )(b † + b) at the antinode
Φ b = S B dS = φ b (b † + b),(7)
φ b = µ 0 s 2π ω b 2L 0 l b 1/2 ln b 1 + b 2 b 2 . (8
div.p: This differs from the conventional JPA driven by a classical field [43].
We then substitute Eqs. ( 6) and ( 7) into the coupling term L (3) S given in Eq. ( 2) with δ = ∆ 1 φ 1 , where
is the fundamental-mode amplitude difference across the SQUID (see Appendix A). The interaction Hamiltonian H I = -L (3) S is then given by
where the nonlinear coupling strength χ depends on Φ e ,
div.formula: χ = tan π Φ e Φ 0 φ b Φ 0 π ∆ 2 1 4L J C Σ ω a .(10)
div.p: In the case of χ {ω a , ω b } and ω b ≈ 2ω a , we can neglect the fast oscillating terms via the rotating-wave approximation (RWA) [53] and the interaction Hamiltonian is reduced to H I = -χ(a † a † b + aab † ). Now, we can write the total Hamiltonian H 0 = H a + H b + H I of the proposed SQC system as
div.formula: H 0 = ω a a † a + ω b b † b -χ(a † a † b + aab † ),(11)
div.p: which is the Hamiltonian for quantized-field-driven PDC.

In another case when the resonant frequency of CWR-A is much larger than that of CWR-B, i.e., ω a ω b , H I in Eq. ( 9) can be approximated as the standard interaction Hamiltonian of an optomechanical system H I = -2χa † a(b † + b) [54]. Here it is not the scope of the present work and will not be investigated. In Ref. [50], the case of x J /l a = ±1 is studied, where the effect of the SQUID is approximated as a turnable length of the SQUID-terminated CWR and the Hamiltonian of two coupled resonators has the standard optomechanical form.

In Fig. 2(a), we plot the nonlinear coupling strength χ versus both the external magnetic flux Φ e /Φ 0 and the position x J /l a of the SQUID. When the SQUID is located at the position near the center of CWR-A and the reduced external flux Φ e /Φ 0 approaches 0.45, i.e., around the bottom right corner of Fig. 2(a), a large coupling strength can be obtained. For clarity, in Fig. 2(b), we also show the results for x J /l a = 0.25, 0.5 and 0.75, corresponding to the three white dashed lines in Fig. 2(a). For x J /l a = 0.25 and Φ e /Φ 0 = 0.45, the nonlinear coupling strength is χ/2π = 18 MHz, approximately three orders of magnitude higher than that achieved in previous studies [44,45]. Since the frequencies of the CWR-A and CWR-B, ω a and ω b , are in the microwave domain (a few gigahertz), the condition χ {ω a , ω b } to make the RWA in Eq. ( 11) is well satisfied. As shown in Sec. III, a larger nonlinear coupling strength can give rise to an appreciable enhancement of the microwave squeezing in the proposed circuit, which reveals the advantage of our system.

The decay rate of a superconducting CWR is usually smaller than 1 × 2π MHz [48]. Therefore, the proposed SQC system can reach the strong-coupling regime, i.e., χ is larger than the decay rates of CWR-A and CWR-B, where the photon blockade, inaccessible in the weak-coupling regime, can be engineered via the quantized-field-driven PDC [49]. In fact, under the conditions of both Φ e Φ b and E J (Φ e ) E c , an even larger coupling strength χ can be achieved by further increasing the static magnetic flux Φ e . Thus, our setup also provides the advantage to demonstrate the photon blockade in a SQC system.

div.head: III. MICROWAVE SQUEEZING ENHANCEMENT
div.p: In this section, we study the performance of our scheme for microwave squeezing. When a microwave field with frequency ω d drives CWR-B, the interaction Hamiltonian is
div.formula: H d = Ω d (b † e -iω d t + be iω d t )
div.p: , where Ω d is the drive strength. In the rotating frame with respect to the drive-field frequency ω d , when ω b = 2ω a and ω d = ω b , the total Hamiltonian H = H 0 + H d of the system can be written as
div.formula: H = -χ(a † a † b + aab † ) + Ω d (b † + b).(12)
div.p: Intuitively, by taking the parametric approximation (i.e., replacing the operators b and b † with their expectation values β and β * ) and removing the constant terms, the above Hamiltonian can be approximated as [44]
div.formula: H ≈ -(χ eff a † a † + χ * eff aa),(13)
div.p: with χ eff = χβ, which is a standard two-photon Hamiltonian for generating the squeezing. To be more precise, here we do not adopt this approximation, but directly harness the Hamiltonian in Eq. ( 12) to study the microwave squeezing of the proposed system. We assume that the CWR-A and CWR-B interact with their respective thermal reservoirs, which are independent from each other. Via the Heisenberg-Langevin approach [53], the equations of motion for the field operators a and b can be writ- ten as
div.formula: ȧ = 2iχa † b - κ a 2 a + F a (t), ḃ = iχaa -iΩ d - κ b 2 b + F b (t),(14)
div.p: where κ a and κ b are the decay rates of CWR-A and CWR-B, respectively, while F a (t) and F b (t) are the related noise operators. In order to linearize Eq. ( 14), we write the operator a (b) as a sum of the expectation value α (β) and the fluctuation δa (δb), i.e., a = α + δa and b = β + δb. It follows from Eq. ( 14) that the expectation values α and β satisfy
div.formula: α = 2iχα * β - κ a 2 α, β = iχα 2 -iΩ d - κ b 2 β. (15
div.p: At the steady state, α = β = 0. Solving Eq. ( 15) with α = β = 0, we obtain two sets of solutions. One set of solutions are
div.formula: α s = 0, β s = -2iΩ d /κ b ,(16)
div.p: and the other set of solutions are
div.formula: α s = ± Ω d (χ -χ c )/χ, β s = -iκ a /4χ,(17)
div.p: which are steady for χ < χ c and χ > χ c [45], respectively, with χ c = κ a κ b /(8Ω d ) being the critical coupling strength.

Due to the occurrence of the second-order phase transition, the microwave-field amplitudes in both CWR-A and CWR-B display abrupt changes at χ/χ c = 1, as shown in Fig. 3(a). When χ/χ c < 1, the absolute values |α s | and |β s |, given by Eq. ( 16), are constants. However, in the case of χ/χ c > 1, as the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c increases, the absolute value |α s |, given by Eq. ( 17), increases from 0 at χ/χ c = 1 to 0.07 around χ/χ c = 2 and then decreases monotonically (blue solid curve), while the absolute value |β s |, also given by Eq. ( 17), monotonically decreases with the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c (red dashed curve).

To further study the dynamics of the system, we plot the time evolution of the absolute values |α| and |β| for various values of the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c in Figs. (b) and 3(c), we find that the evolution of |α| (|β|) remains nearly the same for different values of χ/χ c , indicating that in the case of χ/χ c < 1, the evolutions are independent of the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c and there is no energy exchange between the two resonators. The reason is that the initial state of CWR-A is nearly in its steady state, while the initial state of CWR-B deviates considerably from its steady state. However, when χ is larger than the critical value χ c and the initial states of both CWR-A and CWR-B deviate from their steady states, e.g., χ/χ c =4 in Fig. 3 Below we investigate the microwave squeezing in CWR-A via the variances δx 2 a ≡ x 2 ax a 2 = (δa † + δa) 2 and δy 2 a ≡ y 2 ay a 2 = -(δa † -δa) 2 , where x a = a † + a and y a = i(a †a) are the Hermitian amplitude operators. For clarity, here we only give the main results; detailed derivations can be found in Appendix B. At the steady state, the variances δx 2 a and δy 2 a can be expressed as
div.formula: δx 2 a s =            κ a κ b κ a κ b -8χΩ d , χ < χ c ; 1 + κ a κ b + κ a κ b 2(8χΩ d -κ a κ b ) , χ > χ c ,(18)
div.p: and
div.formula: δy 2 a s =              κ a κ b κ a κ b + 8χΩ d , χ < χ c ; 16χΩ d (κ a + κ b ) -κ a κ 2 b 16χΩ d (2κ a + κ b ) , χ > χ c .(19)
div.p: At the critical point χ/χ c = 1, the variance δy 2 a s has an abrupt change, while the variance δx 2 a s is divergent, which is a characteristic of the second-order phase transition [55]. In Figs. 4(a) and 4(b), we plot the steady-state variances δx 2 a s and δy 2 a s versus the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c (blue solid curves). The steady-state variance δx 2 a s is always larger than 1, while the conjugate variance δy 2 a s is smaller than 1. This implies the emergence of microwave squeezing in CWR-A [44,45]. When χ/χ c < 1, δy 2 a s decreases monotonically from 1 to 0.5, while it increases monotonically from 0.5 to 0.524 in the region of χ/χ c > 1, where the optimum steadystate squeezing is δy 2 a s = 0.5 around the critical point χ/χ c = 1.

Moreover, we also present the time evolution of the variance δy 2 a for different values of the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c in Fig. 4(c). It can be seen that below the critical coupling strength, the variance δy 2 a decreases monotonically with time t to its minimum δy 2 a min (= δy 2 a s ), corresponding to the optimum squeezing (cf. the blue solid and red dashed curves). However, when the coupling strength exceeds the critical value χ c , the variance δy 2 a decreases monotonically with time t to its minimum δy 2 a min (< δy 2 a s ) and then oscillates before reaching the steady-state value δy 2 a s . These dynamical behaviors of the microwave squeezing can be understood using the parametric-approximation Hamiltonian in Eq. ( 13). Below the critical coupling strength, the effective nonlinear coefficient |χ eff | (∝ |β|) increases monotonically with time t [cf. Figs. 3(b) and 3(c)], which is responsible for the monotonic decrease of the variance δy 2 a . When χ/χ c > 1 (i.e., above the critical coupling strength), the oscillatory |χ eff | (∝ |β|) results in the oscillating behavior of the variance δy 2 a [cf. Fig. 3(d)].

In addition, we plot the minimum δy 2 a min versus the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c in Fig. 4(b) (the red dashed curve) as well as the time t min (for reaching δy 2 a min ) versus χ/χ c in Fig. 4(d). Note that δy 2 a decreases monotonically with time t in the region of χ/χ c < 1 and the time t min does not exist [see the gray region in Fig. 4(d)]. Because both δy 2 a min and t min versus χ/χ c decrease monotonically, a larger coupling strength can yield more appreciable and faster squeezing. This promises a considerable enhancement of the microwave squeezing via the quantized-field-driven PDC in our proposed circuit, where the PDC coefficient is strengthened by three orders of magnitude, from tens of kilohertz in Ref. [44,45] to tens of megahertz in the present work. When generating the squeezing, the amplitude of the quantized microwave field in CWR-B is on the order of κ a /χ [cf. Eqs. ( 16)- (17) and Fig. 3], smaller than 1 because the proposed system is in the strong-coupling regime with {κ a , κ b } max < χ. This reveals that microwave squeezing is implemented in the CWR-A, with CWR-B in the quantum limit of single photon.

The above results are obtained at zero temperature, i.e., we take n a = n b = 0 in the correlation functions F † q (t)F q (t ) = n q κ q δ(t -t ) and F q (t)F † q (t ) = (n q +1)κ q δ(t -t ) with q = a, b (cf. Appendix B), where n a and n b are the numbers of thermal average photons in CWR-A and CWR-B. In experiments, the SQCs operate at milli-Kelvin temperatures [56], and the typical frequencies of CWRs are a few gigahertz. If we choose ω a /2π = 3.5 GHz and ω b = 2ω a , the numbers of thermal average photons in CWR-A and CWR-B are about n a = 2.2 × 10 -4 and n b = 5×10 -8 at 20 mK (n a = 1.5×10 -2 and n b = 2.2×10 -4 even at 40 mK), respectively. Therefore, it is reasonable to neglect the temperature effect.

Note that when replacing CWR-B with a transmission line, the system is reduced to a flux-driven JPA, which is a standard device for creating microwave squeezing in the SQCs [56]. For the JPA driven by a classical field via the transmission line, the Hamiltonian of the system can be written as [43] H JPA = iΩ d (a †2a 2 ). For simplicity, we use the same symbol Ω d in both cases. When Ω d /κ a < 0.25, the steady-state value coincides the minimum of the variance δy 2 a , i.e., δy 2 a s = δy 2 a min = κ a /(κ a + 4Ω d ) [53], which decrease monotonically with the drive strength Ω d /κ a , but the JPA becomes unstable for Ω d /κ a > 0.25. Since the JPA can become unstable around the critical point Ω d /κ a = 0.25, δy 2 a s = δy 2 a min = 0.5 is experimentally inaccessible. On the contrary, our proposed system is stable around the critical point and the optimum steadystate squeezing δy 2 a s = 0.5 can be experimentally achievable [cf. Fig. 4(b)]; note that Fig. 4(b) is plotted by fixing the critical coupling strength χ c (related to the drive strength Ω d ) and varying the coupling strength χ. When fixing χ and varying χ c , a similar figure can be obtained. Moreover, the proposed system is stable above the critical drive strength, where the minimum δy 2 a min is smaller than 0.5, which is also beyond the JPA [cf. Fig. 4(b)].

div.head: IV. DISCUSSIONS AND CONCLUSIONS
div.p: In the above study, the higher-order nonlinear effect related to L (4) S is not considered. Below we show that this approximation is reasonable. With the relation in Eq. ( 6), we can obtain the Kerr Hamiltonian H Kerr = -L (4) S = -Ka † aa † a under the RWA, where the Kerr coefficient is

Figure 5(a) displays the ratio of the Kerr coefficient K to the coupling strength χ versus the external flux Φ e /Φ 0 at different positions x J /l a of the SQUID. The corresponding χ versus Φ e /Φ 0 can be found in Fig. 2(b). For different values of x J /l a , the ratio K/χ increases monotonically with Φ e /Φ 0 . Also, for a fixed external flux Φ e /Φ 0 , the ratio K/χ decreases when increasing x J /l a . Based on the results in Figs. 5(a) and 2(b), it is vital to choose appropriate Φ e /Φ 0 and x J /l a to have a stronger coupling strength χ but still keep the ratio K/χ reasonably small. Figures 5(b)-5(d) present the time evolution of the variance δy 2 a in the two cases without and with the Kerr term for different coupling strength χ/χ c (see Appendix B for the detailed calculations). Obviously, the variance δy 2 a shows minor difference in these two cases for various values of χ/χ c (cf. the blue solid and red dashed curves). Therefore, it is reasonable to neglect the effect of the higher-order nonlinearities in our scheme. Moreover, only the microwave squeezing in CWR-A is investigated above. In fact, CWR-B can also exhibit the squeezing effect above the critical point (i.e., χ/χ c > 1), but weaker than CWR-A (see Appendix C).

There are also other theoretical proposals for producing microwave squeezing using, e.g., superconducting resonant tank circuits [57], circuit quantum electrodynamical systems [42,58] and cavity magnomechanical systems [59], but those schemes have not been explored experimentally. Different from those schemes, the JPA is widely studied in the experiment and our proposal provides an improved scheme modified from the flux-driven JPA. With the existing technologies, our proposed circuit can be easily fabricated in the experiment [47]. Besides generating the squeezing, our scheme may have other potential applications in quantum technologies. For example, in our scheme, the nonlinear interaction of quantized-field-driven PDC can be achieved in the strongcoupling regime, because the typical decay rate of the CWR is smaller than 1 × 2π MHz [48] and the strong-coupling condition χ > {κ a , κ b } max can be well satisfied. With this strongcoupling quantized-field-driven PDC, the photon blockade becomes achievable [49], while it is inaccessible in the weakcoupling regime.

In conclusion, we have shown that the microwave squeezing can be considerably enhanced by designing a SQC, with two CWRs coupled via a SQUID embedded in one of the CWRs. In contrast to the flux-driven JPA [43], optimum steady-state squeezing is experimentally accessible around the critical point and stronger transient squeezing is achievable above the critical drive strength in our scheme. Also, compared with the existing schemes of generating microwave squeezing via PDC [44,45], our proposed SQC system can increase the nonlinear coupling strength by three orders of magnitude (i.e., from tens of kilohertz to tens of megahertz). This promises an appreciable enhancement of the microwave squeezing in the resonator.
Eq. (A3) into Eq. (A1) and then performing spatial integration, we can write the Lagrangian L a in the form of a set of harmonic oscillators,
div.formula: L a = m 1 2 C Σ φ2 m - φ 2 m 2L m ,(A10)
div.p: where the relations in Eqs. (A8) and (A9) are used.
Appendix B: Dynamical equations of CWR-A and CWR-B
When including the higher-order nonlinear effect of the SQUID, which corresponds to adding a Kerr term -Ka † aa † a to the Hamiltonian H in Eq. ( 12), the total Hamiltonian of the system becomes
div.formula: H tot = -χ(a † a † b + aab † ) -Ka † aa † a + Ω d (b † + b). (B1)
div.p: To study the microwave squeezing in the system, we define the variances δx
div.formula: 2 o ≡ x 2 o -x o 2 = (δo † + δo)
ȧ = 2iχa † b + iK(aa † a + a † aa) - κ a 2 a + F a (t), ḃ = iχaa -iΩ d - κ b 2 b + F b (t), (B2
div.p: where κ a and κ b are the decay rates of the microwave fields in CWR-A and CWR-B, respectively. Here F a (t) and F b (t) are the corresponding input noise operators, which satisfy F q (t) = 0, F q (t)F q (t ) = F † q (t)F † q (t ) = 0, F † q (t)F q (t ) = n q κ q δ(tt ), and F q (t)F † q (t ) = (n q + 1)κ q δ(tt ), with q = a, b, where n a and n b are the numbers of thermal average photons in CWR-A and CWR-B. Due to the fact that the SQCs operate at milli-Kelvin temperatures [56], it is reasonable to ignore the temperature effect and take n a = n b = 0. Following the procedures in Refs. [45,61], if we write the operators a and b as a = α + δa and b = β + δb, with α (β) being the expectation value and δa (δb) the fluctuation, it follows from Eq. (B2) that the average values α and β satisfy α
div.formula: = 2iχα * β + 2iK|α| 2 α - κ a 2 α, β = iχα 2 -iΩ d - κ b 2 β,(B3)
div.p: and the fluctuation operators δa and δb obey
div.formula: d dt δa = 2iχα * δb + 2(iχβ + iKα 2 )δa † +(4iK|α| 2 -κ a /2)δa + F a (t), d dt δb = 2iχαδa - κ b 2 δb + F b (t),(B4)
div.p: where the high-order terms of the fluctuations have been neglected. In the special case without the Kerr effect (i.e., K = 0), the linearization procedure in Eq. (B4) gives the correct results both below and above the critical point, as discussed in Refs. [45,61]. In our scheme, the Kerr coefficient is much weaker than the coupling strength [cf. Fig. 5(a) and related discussions], and the Kerr effect can be regarded as a perturbation [62]. Thus, the linearized approximation used in Eq. (B4) is well justified.

To investigate the variances of the proposed system, we define the correlation parameters A 1 = δa 2 , A 2 = δa † δa + δaδa † , and A 3 = δa †2 for CWR-A, which are related to the two-operator fluctuations. Using Eq. (B4), we obtain the equations of motion for A 1 , A 2 and A 3 ,
div.formula: Ȧ1 = 4iχα * C 1 + 2(iχβ + iKα 2 )A 2 + 2(4iK|α| 2 -κ a /2)A 1 + δaF a (t) + F a (t)δa , Ȧ2 = 4iχα * C 2 + 4(iχβ + iKα 2 )A 3 + δa † F a (t) + F a (t)δa † + h.c. -κ a A 2 , Ȧ3 = -4iχαC * 1 -2(iχβ * + iKα * 2 )A 2 -2(4iK|α| 2 + κ a /2)A 3 + δa † F † a (t) + F † a (t)δa † ,(B5)
div.p: where C 1 = δaδb and C 2 = δa † δb are the cross-correlation parameters for both CWR-A and CWR-B. Note that all correlation functions involving the noise operators in Eq. (B5) are zero, except for F a (t)a † = aF † a (t) = κ a /2 [53]. Therefore, Eq. (B5) reduces to
div.formula: Ȧ1 = 4iχα * C 1 + 2(iχβ + iKα 2 )A 2 + 2(4iK|α| 2 -κ a /2)A 1 , Ȧ2 = [4iχα * C 2 + 4(iχβ + iKα 2 )A 3 + h.c.] -κ a A 2 + κ a , Ȧ3 = -4iχαC * 1 -2(iχβ * + iKα * 2 )A 2 -2(4iK|α| 2 + κ a /2)A 3 . (B6)
div.p: With similar procedures, the equations of motion for the crosscorrelation parameters C 1 and C 2 are given by
div.formula: Ċ1 = 2iχα * B 1 + 2iχαA 1 + 2(iχβ + iKα 2 )C 2 +(4iK|α| 2 -κ a /2 -κ b /2)C 1 , Ċ2 = -iχα(B 2 -1) + iχα(A 2 -1) -2(iχβ * + iKα * 2 )C 1 -(4iK|α| 2 + κ a /2 + κ b /2)C 2 , (B7
div.p: where the correlation parameters
div.formula: B 1 = δb 2 , B 2 = δb † δb + δbδb † and B 3 = δb †2 for CWR-B satisfy Ḃ1 = 4iχαC 1 -κ b B 1 , Ḃ2 = 4iχαC * 2 -4iχα * C 2 -κ b B 2 + κ b , Ḃ3 = -4iχα * C * 1 -κ b B 3 . (B8)
div.p: Here we assume that the noise operators F a (t) and F b (t) are independent for the two resonators, i.e., F a where the initial states in CWR-A and CWR-B are assumed to be coherent states |α 0 = 0.01 and |β 0 = 0.01i , respectively, which correspond to α(t = 0) = 0.01, β(t = 0) = 0.01i, A 1 (t = 0) = A 3 (t = 0) = B 1 (t = 0) = B 3 (t = 0) = C 1 (t = 0) = C 2 (t = 0) = 0, and A 2 (t = 0) = B 2 (t = 0) = 1.
div.formula: (t)F b (t ) = F † a (t)F † b (t ) = F † a (t)F b (t ) = F a (t)F † b (t ) = 0, under which aF b (t) = a † F b (t) = bF a (t) = bF † a (t) = 0. Ob- viously, the variances δx 2 a , δy2
div.p: In addition to the dynamical behaviors, we are also interested in the steady-state behaviors of the proposed SQC system. Without the Kerr effect (i.e., K = 0), is reduced to Eq. ( 15), and the corresponding steady-state amplitudes α s and β s , which satisfy α s = α * s and β s = -β * s , are given in Eqs. ( 16) and ( 17). Here we assume that the Rabi frequency b for different values of the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c in Fig. 6(b) (the related calculation details can be found in Appendix B). Below the critical coupling strength, i.e., χ/χ c < 1, no microwave squeezing occurs in CWR-B (see the blue solid and red dashed curves). While above the critical coupling strength, i.e., χ/χ c > 1, microwave squeezing occurs in CWR-B (see the green dashed-dotted and magenta dotted curves). By comparing Figs. 6(b) and 4(c), we find that for a given coupling strength χ, CWR-A exhibits a stronger squeezing effect than CWR-B.

figure.head: FIG. 1 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the proposed SQC system consisting of two CWRs denoted as CWR-A and CWR-B, which are nonlinearly coupled to each other via a SQUID (green dot) embedded in CWR-A. (b) An enlarged view of the part denoted by a red rectangular in (a). (c) The lumped-element circuit of CWR-A, where the SQUID is located at x = x J .

figure.head: FIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2. (a) The nonlinear coupling strength χ/2π versus the external flux Φ e /Φ 0 and the position x J /l a of the SQUID. (b) The nonlinear coupling strength χ versus the external flux Φ e /Φ 0 for x J /l a = 0.25, 0.5 and 0.75, which are the cross-section views of (a) indicated by the three white dashed lines. Other parameters are 2l a = 12 mm, 2l b = πυ/(2ω a ), L 0 = 4.5 × 10 -7 H/m, C 0 = 1.8 × 10 -10 F/m, E J /2π = 600 GHz, E C /2π = 1 GHz, s/(2l a ) = 0.05, and b 2 = 0.5b 1 .

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. (a) The microwave-field amplitudes |α s | and |β s | versus the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c , calculated using Eqs. (16) and (17). (b)-(d) The time evolution of the microwave-field amplitudes |α| and |β|, calculated using Eq. (15), where the initial values are α(t = 0) = 0.01 and β(t = 0) = 0.01i, with (b) χ/χ c = 0.3, (c) χ/χ c = 1, and (d) χ/χ c = 4. Since the fabrication of the SQUID may reduce the quality factor of CWR-A by one or two orders of magnitude [43], we choose κ b = 0.1κ a . Other parameters are κ a /2π = 2 MHz and Ω d /2π = 0.03 MHz.

3(b)-3(d). It can be seen that below the critical coupling strength (i.e., χ/χ c < 1), the microwave field in CWR-A is almost in the ground state with |α| ≈ 0 at any time t, but the microwavefield amplitude |β| of CWR-B increases with the time and finally reaches its steady-state value |β s | = 0.3 [see Figs. 3(b) and 3(c)]. By comparing Figs. 3
(d), the time evolutions of |α| and |β| exhibit obvious oscillations before reaching their steady-state values |α s | = 0.06 and |β s | = 0.08, and both CWR-A and CWR-B have energy exchange.

figure.head: FIG. 4 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 4. (a) The steady-state variance δx 2 a s versus the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c . (b) The steady-state variance δy 2 a s and the minimum δy 2 a min versus the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c . (c) The time evolution of the variance δy 2 a for χ/χ c = 0.3, 1, 2 and 4. (d) The time t min versus the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c . Note that no t min exists in the gray region of χ/χ c < 1. Other parameters are the same as in Fig. 3(a).

figure.head: FIG. 5 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 5. (a) The ratio K/χ versus the external flux Φ e /Φ 0 for x J /l a = 0.25, 0.5 and 0.75. Other parameters are the same as in Fig. 2. (b)-(d) The time evolution of the variance δy 2 a in the two cases without and with the Kerr term, respectively, where the reduced coupling strength and the corresponding Kerr coefficient are (b) χ/χ c = 0.3, K/χ = 0.008, (c) χ/χ c = 1, K/χ = 0.013, and (d) χ/χ c = 4, K/χ = 0.027. Here the SQUID are located at x J /l a = 0.75, and the different values of χ and K in (b)-(d) correspond to different external flux Φ e [cf. green dashed-dotted curve in (a)]. Other parameters are the same as in Fig. 3(a).

figure.head: 2 and δy 2 o
figure.figDesc: ≡ y 2 oy o 2 = -(δo † -δo) 2 , with o = a, b, where the Hermitian amplitude operators are given by x o = (o † + o) and y o = i(o †o). Below we derive the variances δx 2 a and δy 2 a for the microwave field in CWR-A, as well as the variances δx 2 b and δy 2 b for the microwave field in CWR-B. With the Hamiltonian in Eq. (B1), the dynamics of the proposed system is governed by the following Heisenberg-Langevin equations:

figure.head: a , δx 2 bFIG. 6 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 6. (a) The steady-state variances δx 2 b s and δy 2 b s versus the reduced coupling strength χ/χ c . (b) The time evolution of the variance δx 2 b for χ/χ c = 0.3, 1, 2 and 4. Other parameters are the same as in Fig. 3(a).

figure.head: δy 2 a-δx 2 b -2iχβ s + κ a + κ b 2 δy a δx b , d dt δx 2 b
figure.figDesc: Ω d is real. Using Eqs. (B6)-(B8) and also with α = α s = α * s and β = β s = -β * s , we obtain the dynamical equations of the variances,d dt δx 2 a = -4χα s δx a δy b + (4iχβ s -κ a ) δx 2 a + κ a , d dt δx a δy b = 2χα s δx 2 a -δy 2 b + 2iχβ s -κ a + κ b 2 δx a δy b , d dt δy 2 b = 4χα s δx a δy b -κ b δy 2 b + κ b , = 4χα s δy a δx b -(4iχβ s + κ a ) δy 2 a + κ a , d dt δy a δx b = -2χα s δy 2 a = -4χα s δy a δx b -κ b δx 2 b + κ b . (B10)Here Eqs. (B9) and (B10) are valid in the limit t → +∞. At the steady states, the steady-state variances δx 2 a s , δy 2 a s , δx 2 b s and δy 2 b s , as given in Eqs. (18), (19), (C1) and (C2), can be derived by solving Eqs. (B9) and (B10) with d δx 2 a /dt = d δx a δy b /dt = d δy 2 b /dt = d δy 2 a /dt = d δy a δx b /dt = d δx 2 b /dt = 0. Appendix C: Microwave squeezing in CWR-B In Sec. III, we have studied the microwave squeezing in CWR-A. Here we further show the microwave squeezing in CWR-B. At the steady state, the variances δx 2 b and δy 2 b are obtained as (see Appendix B) c ; 8χΩ d (κ a + κ b ) + κ 2 a κ b 8χΩ d (2κ a + κ b ) , χ > χ c , c ; (κ a + κ b )/κ b , χ > χ c . (C2) When χ < χ c (i.e., below the critical point), no microwave squeezing occurs in CWR-B, because δx 2 b s = δy 2 b s = 1. However, when the coupling strength χ is larger than the critical value χ c , δx 2 b s < 1 and δy 2 b s > 1 [cf. Fig. 6(a)]. In this region, microwave squeezing can occur in CWR-B. Compared with the steady-state squeezing in CWR-A, the squeezing effect in CWR-B is weaker (i.e., δx 2 b s > δy 2 a s ) for a finite coupling strength χ [cf. Figs. 6(a) and 4(b)]. In the limit χ/χ c → +∞, the two resonators have the same squeezing effect with δy 2 a s = δx 2 b s = 0.524. To further study the squeezing in CWR-B, we plot the time evolution of the variance δx 2

div.head: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
div.p: We acknowledge valuable discussions with Prof. Jian-Qiang You. This work is supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Grant No. 2016YFA0301200), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants No. 11774022, No. U1801661, and No. 11934010), and Zhejiang Province Program for Science and Technology (Grant No. 2020C01019). G.-Q.Z. is supported by the Postdoctoral Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 2020M671687).

div.head: Appendix A: Normal modes of CWR-A
div.p: With the Lagrangian L a of bare CWR-A in Eq. ( 1) and the linear term L (2) S of the Lagrangian of the SQUID in Eq. ( 2), we can obtain the total linear Lagrangian L a = L a + L (2) S ,
According to the Euler-Lagrange equation of motion, the flux field Φ(x, t) is found to obey the wave equation
with the velocity υ = 1/ √ C 0 L 0 . By separating the variables, we write the solution in the form of

where φ m is the flux amplitude of the mth mode of CWR-A and µ m (x) is the corresponding spatial mode function. Substituting Eq. (A3) into Eq. (A2), we obtain two independent ordinary differential equations,
with the wave vector k m and the mode frequency ω m = k m υ.
To satisfy the boundary conditions in Eq. ( 3) and the conditions in Eq. ( 4) for the point x = x J , the general solution of the spatial mode function µ m (x) takes the form
From Eq. ( 3), the phase ϕ 0 = π/2 can be obtained. By substituting the spatial mode function in Eq. (A5) into Eq. ( 4), we get the expression for the relative amplitudes B m ,
and the transcendental equation for the wave vectors k m ,
which can be numerically solved. The normalization constants A m can be fixed with the inner product relation [60]
where

is the mode-amplitude difference across the SQUID, and C Σ = 2C 0 l a + C J is the total capacitance. In addition, from Eqs. (A7) and (A8), the inner product of the envelope derivatives are found to obey a similar orthonormality condition,
where L m defined by 1/L m = C Σ ω 2 m is the effective inductance of the mth mode. Substituting the normal-mode expansion in

Squeezed Vacuum Used to Accelerate the Search for a Weak Classical Signal

Engineering Matter Interactions Using Squeezed Vacuum
Exponentially Enhanced Light-Matter Interaction, Cooperativities, and Steady-State Entanglement Using Parametric Amplification
Squeezed Thermal Reservoirs as a Resource for a Nanomechanical Engine beyond the Carnot Limit
Finite-time performance of a quantum heat engine with a squeezed thermal bath

Quantum squeezing in a modulated optomechanical system
Superconducting Circuits for Quantum Information: An Outlook
Atomic physics and quantum optics using superconducting circuits
Quantum information processing with superconducting circuits: a review
Gate-based superconducting quantum computing
Dressed-state realization of the transition from electromagnetically induced transparency to Autler-Townes splitting in superconducting circuits
Raman coherence in a circuit quantum electrodynamics lambda system
Electromagnetically Induced Transparency in Circuit Quantum Electrodynamics with Nested Polariton States
Optical amplification assisted by two-photon processes in a 3-level transmon artificial atom
Broken selection rule in the quantum Rabi model
Single-photondriven high-order sideband transitions in an ultrastrongly coupled circuit-quantum-electrodynamics system
Generation and Control of Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger Entanglement in Superconducting Circuits

Preserving entanglement during weak measurement demonstrated with a violation of the Bell-Leggett-Garg inequality

Observation of the Bloch-Siegert Shift in a Qubit-Oscillator System in the Ultrastrong Coupling Regime

Exploring the quantum critical behaviour in a driven Tavis-Cummings circuit

Observation of parametric amplification and deamplification in a Josephson parametric amplifier
Theory of degenerate three-wave mixing using circuit QED in solid-state circuits
Inducing Nonclassical Lasing via Periodic Drivings in Circuit Quantum Electrodynamics
Squeezing with a flux-driven Josephson parametric amplifier
Theory of Microwave Parametric Down-Conversion and Squeezing Using Circuit QED

Detection of 15 dB Squeezed States of Light and their Application for the Absolute Calibration of Photoelectric Quantum Efficiency
Tunable and switchable coupling between two superconducting resonators
Hybrid quantum circuits: Superconducting circuits interacting with other quantum systems
Conventional photon blockade with a three-wave mixing
Optomechanicallike coupling between superconducting resonators
Josephson-junction-embedded transmission-line resonators: From Kerr medium to in-line transmon

Microwave photonics with superconducting quantum circuits

monogr.title: Squeezing Microwave Fields via Magnetostrictive Interaction

analytic.title: Langevin equation for the squeezing of light by means of a parametric oscillator
Effect of Higher-Order Nonlinearities on Amplification and Squeezing in Josephson Parametric Amplifiers
